    Problem Statement
    A problem statement is a clear, concise explanation of the problem or challenge you
        intend to solve. It is meant to give you, your team, and any other stakeholders clarity
        and focus about the problem.
    The statement identifies the problem's current state, its desired future state and how to
       close the gap between the two states. It should establish a context for your audience,
       define the problem within this context and propose a solution to the problem.

     What is
                  Design is a process that facilitates problem-solving and
                  innovation. Design is an effective model for identifying and
                  comprehending the relationship between problems and
                  solutions, according to Brown and Wyatt (2010). It presents


     Design
                  a particular way of considering the needs of the people who
                  will consume a product or service and the enabling
                  infrastructure.



     Thinking?
                  Design provides a structured approach to solving complex
                  problems.

                  The processes involved, they explain, enable a more
                  human-centred outcome that is more about the consumer
                  experience than a consumer product (2010).

     Whatever the Problem, Design has the

18




     Why is Design Thinking important for social
     impact?

     "Design thinking is centred on innovating through the eyes of the end
     user and as such encourages in-the-field research that builds empathy
     for people, which results in deeper insights about their unmet
     needs. This focus helps avoid the common problem of enthusiastic
     “outsiders” promoting inappropriate solutions and ensures that
     solutions are rooted in the needs and desires of the community."

     Brown 2011 (Stanford Social Innovation Review)

                                                                                                        1/16/2026




     Double Diamond
     The British Design Council (2019) outlines four core principles for problem-solvers to adopt
     so that they can work as effectively as possible within the Double Diamond framework:

     •   Put people first. Start with an understanding of the people using a service, their needs,
         strengths and aspirations.
     •   Communicate visually and inclusively. Help people gain a shared understanding of
         the problem and ideas.
     •   Collaborate and co-create. Work together and get inspired by what others are doing.
     •   Iterate, iterate, iterate. Do this to spot errors early, avoid risk and build confidence in
         your ideas.

     So what is it?




26




     TWO STAGES - THE DOUBLE DIAMOND




27



                                                                                                             14
                                                                                                                                        1/16/2026




     The Two Diamonds
     The Double Diamond represents two important phases of design.
     The first diamond, "solving the right problem", includes discovering and understanding the
        challenge and defining the challenge further into specifics that can produce ideas to be
        tested.
     The second diamond, "solve the problem right", includes developing ideas and
        prototypes/tests that can be delivered to stakeholders to get feedback and refine these
        further.

     Activity Ideas
     DISCOVER:
     Understand brief, primary research, secondary research, interviewing, empathy mapping, assumptions mapping,
     observation, mind mapping, brainstorming, systems maps.

     DEFINE:
     Cluster learnings, share insights, generate 'how might we' questions, refine brief.

     DEVELOP:
     Ideation, evaluate best ideas (dot-voting and impact/feasibility matrixes), prototype and test, business model
     canvases, integrate feedback.

     DELIVER:
     Refine best ideas, synthesise.

     This is not an exhaustive list. Activities for your schedule will vary depending on the nature of your brief and most
     importantly, your deliverables. Use these as a starting point!

     Develop (Prototype + Test) techniques

                                            concept

44




     Iterate
        Collect feedback from your prototype

        Organize your feedback – are their gaps in your data?

        Return to any stage of the design process

        Adjust to your prototype

        Go back into the field and test again

        Get more feedback




45



                                                                                         23
                                                                                               1/16/2026




     Sustainable funding
       Research the costs for each part of your solution

       Brainstorm funding opportunities – Grants, fundraising, finance, revenue generation?

       Ensure your pitch includes detailed projected long-term financials

       Explore innovative solutions like crowdfunding

       Is there a pricing strategy for your product or service, is it free?

       Assess funding sources for sustainable reliability




46




     Scale
       Assess the metrics from your prototype or current solution

       Conduct secondary research about potential markets or users

       What is the impact of your current solution?

       What is the viability of potential growth?

       What is the feasibility of growth?

       Design a larger scale prototype to learn from




47

                                              Pitch your     Funding        Getting
                                                 idea        Strategy      feedback




                                             Sustainable      Build a     Collaborate
                                              Revenue          team




48




     Pitching your concept
       Define the core idea of your product, experience or service.

       What is your call to action?

       Be clear and unambiguous - focus on the impact

       Think about your audience – what do they need to know?

       Write your pitch in appropriate format – presentation, story, video, report.

       Practice your delivery or test out your pitch

         Leadership is a very
         complex concept that is
         often confused with
         charisma and the use of
         power and authority. The
         truth is that it is all about
         enabling other people to
         be successful – and that
         requires, first, treating
         every person with
         unconditional respect
         and, second, the use of a
         very broad set of
         behaviours that enable
         people to grow and
         develop. It is about
         engaging with people.

                     comprises your Assessment 2. This is due on February 4.
